physical_anchor: Tall, broad-shouldered Avar mountaineer of powerful but lean build, a shaven head under a tall sheepskin papakha and a close-trimmed reddish-black beard framing a slightly pock-marked face with quick, light-colored eyes; carries himself erect in a long cherkeska, moves with a pronounced limp from an old wound, and habitually rests a hand on the hilt of his kinjal or draws his palms down his beard after speaking.
vocal_quality: Low, dry baritone with a guttural Avar-Turkic accent, measured tempo and long pauses, formal in register and almost never raised — authority carried by stillness rather than volume.
social_presence: Enters a room quietly and immediately becomes its center of gravity: other men stand, voices drop, and even Russian officers feel themselves watched by a calm, unblinking warrior whose courtesy is exact and whose reputation does the intimidating for him.
historical_reference: Grounded in contemporary 1851–1852 Russian sources: the portrait and description by G. G. Gagarin, the watercolor sketches and notes of officers at Tiflis and Nukha, and the memoir accounts of A. I. Poltoratsky and M. T. Loris-Melikov (who interviewed him during his stay with the Russians). These sources consistently record a tall, powerfully built Avar of about forty-seven, a shaven head worn under a papakha, a short reddish beard, a slightly pock-marked face with pale, alert eyes, a marked limp from a wound sustained leaping from a cliff during his escape from Akhulgo, and a reserved, ceremoniously polite bearing.
